Name: spectral_layer_skipping
Title: Training-Free Adaptive Layer Skipping via Spectral Stability Diagnostics
Short Hypothesis: Spectral diagnostics derived from activation covariance can predict task-specific computational requirements, enabling adaptive layer skipping to enforce strict latency budgets without retraining.
Related Work: Existing work focuses on spectral-based subspace projection (Spectral Latent Steering) and geometric interference cancellation (Training-Free Geometric Interference Cancellation). This proposal distinguishes by introducing adaptive layer skipping based on spectral stability diagnostics, targeting multi-task inference scenarios.
Abstract: Training-free adaptive layer skipping offers a promising approach to enforce strict latency budgets in multi-task inference scenarios. We propose a novel framework that leverages spectral diagnostics derived from activation covariance to predict task-specific computational requirements. By analyzing the spectral condition number and decay rate of hidden states, we dynamically route tokens through fewer layers when task stability exceeds a calibrated threshold. This approach ensures deterministic latency bounds while maintaining task-specific accuracy. We theoretically ground our framework with PAC-Bayes bounds linking spectral conditioning to generalization stability under layer skipping. Evaluated on multi-task benchmarks, our method achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic layer skipping baselines. By replacing qualitative fragility claims with formalized risk bounds, our framework provides a transparent, reproducible pathway for resource-efficient inference in multi-task scenarios.
Experiments: Evaluate spectral diagnostics on multi-task benchmarks (e.g., GLUE, SuperGLUE) to predict task-specific computational requirements.
Implement adaptive layer skipping based on spectral stability thresholds and compare against static and heuristic baselines.
Measure throughput-accuracy Pareto frontiers and latency distributions across tasks.
Validate PAC-Bayes bounds linking spectral conditioning to generalization stability under layer skipping.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Spectral diagnostics may not generalize well to tasks with highly non-linear activation patterns.
Adaptive layer skipping may introduce additional computational overhead for diagnostics.
The framework assumes tasks exhibit distinct spectral characteristics, which may not hold in all scenarios.
